p_question-11298.ja:
以下は、留学生の作文である。


スキー旅行

チュラノン　ソムチャイ

　冬になると、駅の広告や旅行会社のちらしでスキー旅行の案内をよく見ます。去年の冬、 私はスキーが上手な友達からスキー旅行に誘われました。私は寒いのが苦手だし、スキー もしたことがなかったので迷いました。でも、友達と旅行には行きたかったので、スキー にチャレンジしてみることにしました。

　私たちはバスでスキー旅行に行きました。その日は晴れていましたが、バスを降りたら とても寒くて、私は暖かいところにいたいと思いました。　[19]、せっかく来たのだか らと思って、スキーの練習を始めました。スキーの練習をしていると、すぐに寒さを感じ なくなりました。スキーは難しかったですが、面白かったです。半日で、あまり転ばずに [20]。山の上から滑って降りるときのスピードにどきどきしました。

　今は駅でスキーの広告を見るたびに、[21]スキー旅行を思い出します。一度スキー の面白さを知ってしまうと、また行きたくなります。自分でもスキーをしてみて、日本で スキーが冬の代表的なスポーツになっている理由がよく[22]。

The following is an essay by an international student.


Ski Trip

Chulanon Somchai

　When winter comes, I often see information for ski trips in station advertisements and travel agency flyers. Last winter, I was invited on a ski trip by a friend who is good at skiing. I dislike the cold and had never skied before, so I hesitated. However, because I wanted to go on a trip with my friend, I decided to try challenging myself to ski.

　We went on the ski trip by bus. It was sunny that day, but when we got off the bus it was very cold, and I thought I wanted to be in a warm place. [19], thinking that since I had come all this way, I started ski practice. When practicing skiing, I stopped feeling the cold immediately. Skiing was difficult, but interesting. In half a day, [20] without falling much. My heart pounded at the speed when sliding down from the top of the mountain.

　Now, every time I see ski advertisements at the station, I [21] remember that ski trip. Once you know the fun of skiing, you want to go again. Having tried skiing myself, I [22] well the reason why skiing has become a representative winter sport in Japan.

p_question-11302.ja:
(1)

これは、同じ会社の木村さんからジョンさんに届いたメールである。


ジョンさん


先週は、製品発表会の英語資料のチェックをしてくれて、ありがとうございました。

チェックしてもらったあと、少し文章を書き加えました。それから、直したほうがいいと

アドバイスをもらったグラフも作り直しました。

直した資料をこのメールと一緒に送りますので、もう一度見てもらえませんか。新しく書

き加えた部分は、赤い字になっています。

すみませんが、今週中にご返信をお願いいたします。


木村

p_question-11303.ja:
(2)

これは、ある図書館のホームページに載っているお知らせである。


緑図書館

1月25日

図書館の休館と特別窓口のお知らせ

　　緑図書館は、4月1日から9月30日まで、工事のため休館することになりま した。この期間は、水森駅の駅ビル「グラン」3階に特別窓口を開きます。

　　特別窓口では、予約した本を借りることと本を返すことができます。本や新 聞を読む場所はありません。なお、本の予約は、インターネットでの予約が便 利ですが、特別窓口でも可能です。 　

　　ご利用可能時間は、決まりましたら、ホームページでお知らせします。 　

　　ご不便をおかけしますが、よろしくお願いいたします。

(2)

This is a notice posted on a certain library's homepage.


Midori (Green) Library

January 25

Notice regarding Library Closure and Special Counter

　　Midori Library will be closed for construction from April 1st to September 30th. During this period, we will open a special counter on the 3rd floor of the Mizumori Station building "Grand".

　　At the special counter, you can borrow reserved books and return books. There is no space to read books or newspapers. Furthermore, while reserving books via the internet is convenient, it is also possible at the special counter. 　

　　We will announce the available service hours on the homepage once they are decided. 　

　　We apologize for the inconvenience and thank you for your cooperation.

p_question-11304.ja:
(3)

　昨日、雑誌で「紙から作られた靴下」という記事を読んだ。そのタイトルを見たとき、 私は、紙コップのように、一度使ったら捨ててしまう物なのだろうと思った。しかし、記 事によると、その靴下は、特別な紙で作った丈夫な糸でできているそうだ。紙なのに水に 強く、洗濯をして何度も使えると書かれていた。予想と違っていて、意外だった。よく汗 を吸ってすぐ乾くので、長い時間靴をはく人にいいそうだ。今度、買ってみようと思う。

(3)

　Yesterday, I read an article in a magazine about "Socks made from paper." When I saw that title, I thought they would be things you throw away after using once, like paper cups. However, according to the article, those socks are made of strong thread created from special paper. It was written that even though they are paper, they are strong against water and can be washed and used many times. It was different from my expectations and surprising. They absorb sweat well and dry quickly, so they are apparently good for people who wear shoes for a long time. I think I'll try buying them next time.

p_question-11305.ja:
(4) 　

　体が痛くて病院に通っている患者が、悪いところが治ってきたときに、「別のところが痛くなってきた。今度はそっちが悪いみたいだ。」と言うことがある。このような場合、 実は、その人には最初から悪いところがいくつかあった可能性がある。人の体は、同じと きに一つの痛みしか感じない。そのときにいちばん強い痛みだけを感じるのである。この 患者は、最も悪かったところが治ってきたので、次の痛みを感じるようになってきたと考 えられる。

(4) 　

　When a patient going to the hospital for body pain starts to heal in the bad area, they sometimes say, "A different place has started hurting. It seems like that side is bad this time." In such cases, actually, there is a possibility that the person had several bad places from the beginning. The human body only feels one pain at the same time. It feels only the strongest pain at that moment. It is thought that because this patient's worst part healed, they came to feel the next pain.

p_question-11306.ja:
(1) 　

　私と妹は、顔はとても似ていると言われますが、嫌いな物や嫌なことに対する姿勢は逆 です。例えば、食事で苦手な物があったとき、私は最後に食べますが、妹は最初に食べて しまいます。

　もう一つ①例があります。私たちが通っていた小学校では、毎年夏休みに宿題がたくさん 出ました。妹はいつもすぐに終わらせましたが、私は夏休みが終わる数日前になって慌 てやり始めました。それで、毎年、私だけ両親にしかられました。

　しかし、ある年、②妹もしかられたことがありました。その年も、妹は夏休みが始まって すぐに、すべての宿題をやってしまいました。そのときは、両親は妹を褒めていました。 でも、両親は、その中に「今年の夏休みの思い出」という作文の宿題があったことに、気 がついていなかったのです。夏休みの最後の日、母は作文の内容がうそであることに気づ いて、書き直させました。

　その日は、私も妹も、しかられながら机に向かいました

(1) 　

　People say my younger sister and I look very alike in the face, but our attitudes toward things we dislike or unpleasant things are opposite. For example, when there is food we don't like, I eat it last, but my sister eats it first.

　There is one more ①example. At the elementary school we attended, a lot of homework was assigned every summer vacation. My sister always finished it immediately, but I would panic and start doing it a few days before the summer vacation ended. So, every year, only I was scolded by our parents.

　However, there was one year when ②my sister was also scolded. That year too, my sister did all her homework right after summer vacation started. At that time, our parents were praising my sister. But, our parents hadn't realized that among that homework, there was a composition assignment titled "Memories of This Summer Vacation." On the last day of summer vacation, our mother realized the content of the composition was a lie and made her rewrite it.

　That day, both my sister and I faced our desks while being scolded.

p_question-11309.ja:
(2) 　

　カラスは、人間の食べ物を奪ったりするので、嫌われることが多い鳥だ。そのカラスに ついて、ある問題を解決した人のニュースを見た。

　ある大学の研究所の建物に、倉庫として使っている場所がある。そこに、カラスが入っ てきて物を壊したり汚したりするので、研究所の人は困っていた。それで、カラスの研究 者に相談したら、「カラス侵入禁止」（注）と書いた紙をはったらどうかと言われた。最初は①冗談だと思ったが、実際にやってみると、②本当に来なくなったそうだ。 　

　カラスに文字が読めるはずがないのに、なぜこの紙に効果があったのだろうか。この研 究者によると、まず、そこを通って紙を見た人が不思議に思って、近くにいるカラスをよ く見る。すると、カラスは危険を感じて来なくなる。カラスは人間に見られるのを嫌うの だそうだ。

　ニュースを見て私は、この研究者がカラスの行動だけでなく、人間の行動についても、 しっかり③予想できていたのだろうと感心した。

（注）侵入禁止：立ち入り禁止

(2) 　

　Crows are birds that are often disliked because they steal human food and such. I saw news about a person who solved a certain problem regarding those crows.

　There is a place being used as a warehouse in a certain university research institute building. People at the institute were troubled because crows would come in there and break or dirty things. So, when they consulted a crow researcher, they were told, "Why don't you post a paper that says 'Crow Entry Prohibited' (Note)?" At first, ①they thought it was a joke, but when they actually did it, ②they heard the crows really stopped coming. 　

　Even though crows shouldn't be able to read writing, why was this paper effective? According to this researcher, first, people passing by who saw the paper would think it strange and look closely at the crows nearby. Then, the crows would feel danger and stop coming. Apparently, crows dislike being watched by humans.

　Watching the news, I was impressed that this researcher was able to firmly ③predict not only the crows' behavior but also human behavior.

（Note）Entry prohibited: Do not enter

p_question-11312.ja:
先週、実家に帰ったとき、両親が、近所に新しくできた魚料理のレストランに連れて 行ってくれた。両親はもう何回か行ったのだそうだ。

　メニューには、よく知られた魚を使った料理はほとんどなく、聞いたことのない魚の料 理が並んでいた。いろいろな料理を食べたが、どれもおいしかった。両親は、この店では 日本で今まで一般的に売られていなかった種類の魚を扱っているのだと教えてくれた。最 近、そのような魚は「未利用魚」と呼ばれ、関心を持つ人が増えているそうだ。 　

　食事のあと、店長がテーブルに来て、いろいろ説明してくれた。日本では、魚がとれる 量が減り続けていて、多いときの半分以下になっている。日本人がよく食べている魚は80 種類ぐらいだが、実は、日本の近くの海では3,000種類以上の魚がとれる。そこで、未利用魚を食べる人を増やそうという動きが広がってきた。食べる人が増えれば、今まで商品 としての価値がなかった魚が売れるようになるので、漁業（注）を応援することになるそうだ。

　未利用魚のことを知らない人は多い。それで、この店では、未利用魚のおいしさや、食 べることの意味を知ってもらうことが重要だと考えているそうだ。私もなるほどと思った し、実は、両親も同じように感じて私を誘ったらしい。

　この話を聞いたあと、私は自分でも買いたいと思って近所のスーパーを見てみたが、未利用魚は売っていなかった。レストランの店長は、最近は未利用魚を缶詰などにしたもの がインターネットで買えると言っていた。せっかく興味を持ったのだから、どういうもの が買えるのか調べてみようと思う。

（注）漁業：魚をとる仕事

Last week, when I returned to my parents' home, my parents took me to a fish restaurant that newly opened in the neighborhood. I heard my parents have already gone there several times.

　On the menu, there were almost no dishes using well-known fish; instead, dishes with fish I had never heard of were lined up. I ate various dishes, and all of them were delicious. My parents taught me that this shop handles types of fish that haven't generally been sold in Japan until now. Recently, such fish are called "unutilized fish," and I hear the number of interested people is increasing. 　

　After the meal, the store manager came to the table and explained various things to us. In Japan, the amount of fish caught continues to decrease, becoming less than half of what it was at its peak. There are about 80 types of fish that Japanese people eat often, but actually, over 3,000 types of fish can be caught in the seas near Japan. Therefore, a movement to increase the number of people eating unutilized fish has spread. If the number of people eating them increases, fish that previously had no commercial value will be able to be sold, so it seems this will support the fishing industry (Note).

　There are many people who do not know about unutilized fish. Therefore, this shop seems to believe it is important to have people know the deliciousness of unutilized fish and the meaning of eating them. I thought "I see," and actually, it seems my parents felt the same way and invited me.

　After hearing this story, I thought I wanted to buy them myself and looked at a neighborhood supermarket, but unutilized fish were not being sold. The restaurant manager was saying that recently, things like canned unutilized fish can be bought on the internet. Since I've taken an interest, I think I will look up what kind of things I can buy.

（Note）Fishing industry: The job of catching fish

questions.stem: 急に涙が出てしまいました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It reads **なみだ (namida)**.

**Question Translation**
“Suddenly, **tears** came out.” (I suddenly started crying.)

**Explanation**
The kanji **涙** means "tears."

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **あせ** (ase) means "sweat" (Kanji: 汗).
3. **せき** (seki) means "cough" (Kanji: 咳).
4. **くしゃみ** (kushami) means "sneeze" (Kanji: 嚏 - often written in Kana).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Words related to physical reactions often appear as a set in N3. Memorize these together: 涙 (tears), 汗 (sweat), 血 (blood - chi), 咳 (cough), くしゃみ (sneeze).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「涙(なみだ)」 — 눈물. 「涙が出る」(눈물이 나다).
오답: 2 あせ는 「汗」(땀), 3 せき는 「咳」(기침), 4 くしゃみ는 「くしゃみ」(재채기).
포인트: 涙=なみだ. 감정 표현과 자주 결합.

questions.stem: けがは完全に治りました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It reads **かんぜん (kanzen)**.

**Question Translation**
“My injury has **completely** healed.”

**Explanation**
The word **完全** means "perfect" or "complete." It combines 完 (completion) + 全 (all).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **かんたん** (kantan) means "simple" (Kanji: 簡単).
3. **けんぜん** (kenzen) means "healthy/sound" (Kanji: 健全).
4. **けんたん** is not a common word.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
When you see a two-character compound (Jukugo), look at the individual radicals. 完 (complete) vs 関 (connection). Don't just guess based on shape; focus on the meaning of the components.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「完全(かんぜん)」 — 완전. 「完全に治る」(완전히 낫다).
오답: 2 かんたん은 「簡単」(간단), 3 けんぜん은 「健全」(건전), 4 けんたん은 없는 음.
포인트: 完=カン, 全=ゼン.

questions.stem: 雨で地面がぬれている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It reads **じめん (jimen)**.

**Question Translation**
“The **ground** is wet from the rain.”

**Explanation**
**地面** means "ground." Here, 地 is read as **ji** (with rendaku voicing) + 面 (men).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **ちめい** (chimei) means "place name" (Kanji: 地名).
2. **ちめん** is an incorrect reading.
3. **じめい** is an incorrect reading.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Be careful with the kanji **地**. It can be read as **chi** (e.g., 地下 - chika, 地図 - chizu) or **ji** (e.g., 地面 - jimen, 地震 - jishin). Memorize words as whole units rather than just the kanji reading.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「地面(じめん)」 — 지면, 땅바닥. 「地面がぬれる」(땅이 젖다).
오답: 1 ちめい는 「地名」(지명), 2·3은 없는 음.
포인트: 地는 「ち/じ」 둘 다 가능 — 「地面」은 「じ」로 읽는 관용.

questions.stem: お金はあの箱に移したほうがいいですか。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It reads **うつした (utsushita)**.

**Question Translation**
“Should I **move/transfer** the money to that box?”

**Explanation**
**移す (utsusu)** means "to move" or "to transfer" (location/infect a disease).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **もどした** (modoshita) means "returned/put back" (Kanji: 戻した).
2. **かくした** (kakushita) means "hid" (Kanji: 隠した).
3. **のこした** (nokoshita) means "left behind/saved" (Kanji: 残した).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
If you are unsure of the Kanji reading, look at the context. "To that box" suggests movement. Also, distinguishing between similar verbs like 移す (move location) and 写す (copy/photograph) is a common N3 trick—both are read *utsusu*.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「移(うつ)した」 — 옮기다.
오답: 1 もどした는 「戻した」(되돌리다), 2 かくした는 「隠した」(숨기다), 3 のこした는 「残した」(남기다).
포인트: 「移す」=장소·물건을 옮기다.

questions.stem: ゆっくり呼吸をしてください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It reads **こきゅう (kokyuu)**.

**Question Translation**
“Please **breathe** slowly.”

**Explanation**
**呼吸** means "breath" or "respiration." 呼 (exhale) + 吸 (inhale).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **こきょう** (kokyou) means "hometown" (Kanji: 故郷).
3. **こうきゅう** (koukyuu) means "high class" (Kanji: 高級).
4. **こうきょう** (koukyou) means "public" (Kanji: 公共).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Pay close attention to **long vowel sounds** (ou vs o, yuu vs yu). 呼吸 is short *Ko* + long *Kyuu*. Incorrectly guessing the vowel length is the most common mistake here.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「呼吸(こきゅう)」 — 호흡.
오답: 1 こきょう는 「故郷」(고향), 3 こうきゅう는 「高級」, 4 こうきょう는 「公共」.
포인트: 呼(コ)+吸(キュウ). 장음 없음에 주의.

questions.stem: ここに身長を書いてください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It reads **しんちょう (shinchou)**.

**Question Translation**
“Please write your **height** here.”

**Explanation**
**身長** refers to body height. 身 (body - shin) + 長 (length - chou).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **せんちょう** (senchou) means "captain" (Kanji: 船長).
3. **じんちょう** is an incorrect reading.
4. **ぜんちょう** (zenchou) means "total length" (Kanji: 全長).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
When you see 身 (body), the On-yomi is usually **shin** (e.g., 独身 - dokushin). When you see 長 (long/leader), the On-yomi is **chou** (e.g., 社長 - shachou).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「身長(しんちょう)」 — 신장, 키.
오답: 1 せんちょう는 「船長」(선장), 3 じんちょう는 없는 음, 4 ぜんちょう는 「前兆」(전조).
포인트: 身=シン, 長=チョウ.

questions.stem: 前田さんは困っているようだ。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It reads **こまって (komatte)**.

**Question Translation**
“Mr. Maeda seems to be **troubled/in a bind**.”

**Explanation**
**困る (komaru)** means "to be troubled," "to be perplexed," or "to have a hard time."

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **まよって** (mayotte) means "to get lost/waver" (Kanji: 迷って).
2. **おこって** (okotte) means "to get angry" (Kanji: 怒って).
4. **だまって** (damatte) means "to be silent" (Kanji: 黙って).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Kanji mnemonics help here: The Kanji 困 looks like a **tree (木)** trapped inside a **box (口)**. It’s stuck and can't grow -> "Troubled".
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「困(こま)って」 — 곤란하다.
오답: 1 まよって는 「迷って」(헤매다), 2 おこって는 「怒って」(화나다), 4 だまって는 「黙って」(잠자코).
포인트: 困る=곤란하다·난처하다.

questions.stem: 近くに薬局はありますか。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It reads **やっきょく (yakkyoku)**.

**Question Translation**
“Is there a **pharmacy** nearby?”

**Explanation**
**薬局** means "pharmacy." Note the small 'tsu' (gemination): *Yaku* + *Kyoku* becomes *Yakkyoku*.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1, 2, 4. These are incorrect readings. They miss either the small 'tsu' or the final 'ku'.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Sound changes (like *Yaku* -> *Yak-*) are very common in N3 compound words. Practice saying the word aloud; "Yakkyoku" flows better than "Yakukyoku."
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「薬局(やっきょく)」 — 약국.
오답: 1·2·4는 모두 촉음(っ)이나 장음을 빠뜨린 잘못된 읽기.
포인트: 薬(ヤク)+局(キョク) → 촉음화 「やっきょく」.

questions.stem: この町で子どもをそだてたいと思っている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. The kanji is **育てたい**.

**Question Translation**
“I am thinking I want to **raise** my children in this town.”

**Explanation**
**育てる (sodateru)** means "to raise" or "to rear." The Kanji **育** relates to upbringing or education.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **産** relates to "giving birth" (産む - umu).
3. **愛** means "love" (ai).
4. **幸** means "happiness" (shiawase).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Distinguish between "Giving birth" (産む) and "Raising" (育てる). The sentence says "in this town," implying a long-term process of raising, so 育 is the correct choice.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「育(そだ)てたい」 — 키우다, 기르다.
오답: 1 産은 「うむ」(낳다), 3 愛, 4 幸은 「そだてる」로 읽지 않음.
포인트: 育てる=양육·교육. 「子どもを育てる」가 정형.

questions.stem: 物が燃えるときに、けむりが出ます。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. The kanji is **煙**.

**Question Translation**
“When things burn, **smoke** comes out.”

**Explanation**
**けむり (kemuri)** means "smoke." The kanji is **煙**. It has the Fire radical (火) on the left.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **燥** is used in 乾燥 (kansou - drying).
3. **焼** is used in 焼く (yaku - to burn/cook).
4. **爆** is used in 爆発 (bakuhatsu - explosion).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
All options here share the **Fire radical (火)**, which makes this a visual trap. You must memorize the right-hand side of the Kanji. 煙 (smoke) has the element on the right that looks like West (西) and Earth (土).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「煙(けむり)」 — 연기.
오답: 2 燥는 「乾燥(かんそう)」, 3 焼는 「焼く(やく)」, 4 爆는 「爆発(ばくはつ)」.
포인트: 火 변(灬)이 단서. 燃える(타다)와 짝.

questions.stem: ここからしんりんが見えます。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. The kanji is **森林**.

**Question Translation**
“You can see the **forest** from here.”

**Explanation**
**森林 (shinrin)** means "forest." It combines the kanji for "Forest" (森 - mori) and "Woods" (林 - hayashi).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **森村** - Not a standard word for forest.
3. **山林** - Reads **さんりん (sanrin)** meaning "mountain forest."
4. **山村** - Reads **さんそん (sanson)** meaning "mountain village."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Match the reading to the Kanji Onyomi. Shin (森) + Rin (林). If you chose 山 (San), the reading would have been different.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「森林(しんりん)」 — 삼림.
오답: 1 森村은 단어 아님, 3 山林은 「さんりん」, 4 山村은 「さんそん」.
포인트: 森(シン)+林(リン). 숲을 가리키는 일반어.

questions.stem: 朝、シャワーをあびました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. The kanji is **浴びました**.

**Question Translation**
“I **took** a shower in the morning.”

**Explanation**
**浴びる (abiru)** means "to bathe" or "to bask in." It is the standard verb for showers (シャワーを浴びる).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **流** is used for "flow" (流れる - nagareru).
2. **洗** is used for "wash" (洗う - arau).
3. **清** is used for "pure/clean" (清い - kiyoi).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
This is a **collocation** question. You "wash" (洗う) your face, but you "bathe in" (浴びる) a shower.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「浴(あ)びました」 — (샤워를) 하다.
오답: 1 流, 2 洗, 3 清는 「あびる」로 읽지 않음.
포인트: 「シャワーを浴びる」가 고정 표현.

questions.stem: 文字の色を変えることはかのうでしょうか。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. The kanji is **可能**.

**Question Translation**
“Is it **possible** to change the text color?”

**Explanation**
**可能 (kanou)** means "possible." It combines 可 (acceptable/can) + 能 (ability).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **可態** - Incorrect combination.
3. **化能** - 化 means "change" (bake), not used here.
4. **化態** - Non-existent word.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Remember opposites. The opposite of 可能 is **不可能** (impossible). Associating words with their antonyms helps recall the correct Kanji.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「可能(かのう)」 — 가능.
오답: 2 可態, 3 化能, 4 化態는 모두 존재하지 않는 조합.
포인트: 可(カ)+能(ノウ). 부정형은 「不可能」.

questions.stem: 今からメールをそうしんします。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. The kanji is **送信**.

**Question Translation**
“I will **send/transmit** the email now.”

**Explanation**
**送信 (soushin)** means "sending" (an email/signal). 送 (send) + 信 (message/trust).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **返信** (henshin) means "reply."
2. **送真** - Incorrect kanji pairing.
4. **返真** - Non-existent word.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Common email vocabulary: **送信** (Send), **受信** (Receive), **返信** (Reply), **転送** (Forward). Memorize these as a set.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「送信(そうしん)」 — 송신.
오답: 1 返信(へんしん)은 회신, 2·4는 존재하지 않는 조합.
포인트: 送(ソウ)+信(シン). 메일·신호를 보낼 때.

questions.stem: 山の上から見た景色が素晴らしくて、とても（　　　）した。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means **moved/touched**.

**Question Translation**
“The scenery seen from the top of the mountain was wonderful, and I was very **moved**.”

**Explanation**
**感動 (kandou)** means "being moved emotionally" or "impressed." It fits the context of seeing beautiful scenery.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **拝見 (haiken)** - Humble form of "to see." Incorrect grammar and meaning here.
2. **歓迎 (kangei)** - "Welcome" (e.g., welcoming a guest).
3. **応答 (outou)** - "Response/Reply" (to a call/question).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Look for the cause and effect. Scenery is wonderful -> Effect on emotion. **感動** is the only emotional reaction in the list.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「感動(かんどう)」 — 감동.
근거: 멋진 경치를 보고 마음이 움직인 상태.
오답: 1 拝見(보다의 겸양), 2 歓迎(환영), 3 応答(응답)은 문맥 불일치.
포인트: 「素晴らしい〜に感動する」가 정형.

questions.stem: この店では、以前誰かが使っていた（　　　）の車を売っている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means **second-hand/used**.

**Question Translation**
“This shop sells **used** cars that someone used before.”

**Explanation**
**中古 (chuuko)** refers to "second-hand" or "used" goods. **中古車 (chuukousha)** is a very common word for used cars.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **後半 (kouhan)** - "Second half" (of a game/year).
2. **中年 (chuunen)** - "Middle age" (of a person).
4. **後期 (kouki)** - "Latter period" (of a semester/era).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
All options start with **中 (middle)** or **後 (after)**, testing your knowledge of specific compounds. "Used goods" is always **中古**.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「中古(ちゅうこ)」 — 중고.
근거: 「以前誰かが使っていた車」(전에 누가 쓰던 차) → 중고차.
오답: 1 後半(후반), 2 中年(중년), 4 後期(후기)는 문맥 어긋남.
포인트: 「中古車」(중고차)는 빈출 어휘.

questions.stem: 来週の旅行に持っていく物の（　　　）を作った。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means **list**.

**Question Translation**
“I made a **list** of things to take on next week's trip.”

**Explanation**
A **リスト (list)** is what you make when checking off items to bring.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **スケジュール** - "Schedule" (time plan).
3. **カタログ** - "Catalog" (book of products).
4. **プログラム** - "Program" (event plan or software).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Identify the object being acted upon. "Things to bring" (持っていく物) -> List. "Time/Places to visit" -> Schedule.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「リスト」 — 리스트, 목록.
근거: 여행에 가지고 갈 물건들을 정리한 것 → 리스트.
오답: 1 スケジュール(시간 계획), 3 カタログ(상품 책자), 4 プログラム(프로그램)은 모두 어긋남.

questions.stem: この前は試合に負けて（　　　）が、今回は勝てたので、本当にうれしい。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It means **frustrated/regrettable**.

**Question Translation**
“Last time I lost the match and was **frustrated**, but this time I won, so I am really happy.”

**Explanation**
**悔しい (kuyashii)** is the specific emotion for "regret/frustration" when you fail or lose despite trying.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **貧しい (mazushii)** - "Poor" (financially).
3. **緩い (yurui)** - "Loose" (clothing, rules).
4. **狡い (zurui)** - "Sly/Cheating."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Keywords: "Lost the match" (負けて) matches with "Kuyashii". "Won" (勝った) matches with "Ureshii".
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「くやしかった」 — 분했다, 억울했다.
근거: 시합에 졌을 때 느끼는 감정.
오답: 2 まずしい(가난한), 3 ゆるい(느슨한), 4 ずるい(교활한)는 문맥 어긋남.
포인트: 「悔しい」=노력했지만 졌을 때의 후회·분함.

questions.stem: ここから市役所までの（　　　）はどのくらいですか。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means **distance**.

**Question Translation**
“How much is the **distance** from here to the City Hall?”

**Explanation**
**距離 (kyori)** means distance. The phrase "どのくらい" (how much) asks for a measurement, fitting distance.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **途中 (tochuu)** - "On the way."
2. **徒歩 (toho)** - "Walking" (method).
3. **方向 (houkou)** - "Direction" (You would ask "Which direction", not "How much direction").

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Check the interrogative word. "Which" (どの/どっち) fits Direction. "How much" (どのくらい) fits Distance.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「距離(きょり)」 — 거리.
근거: 「どのくらい」(얼마쯤)와 짝짓는 것은 거리.
오답: 1 途中(도중), 2 徒歩(도보), 3 方向(방향)은 부적절.
포인트: 「AからBまでの距離」가 정형.

questions.stem: シャワーの後は、このタオルで体を（　　　）ください。

**Question Translation**
“After the shower, please **wipe** your body with this towel.”

**Explanation**
**拭く (fuku)** means to wipe or dry something with a cloth/towel.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **干して (hoshite)** - "To air dry/hang" (laundry).
3. **張って (hatte)** - "To stick/stretch."
4. **直して (naoshite)** - "To fix/correct."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Associate tools with verbs. Towel (タオル) -> Wipe (拭く). Laundry (洗濯物) -> Hang/Dry (干す).
questions.expl_ko:

questions.stem: 配達が遅れたせいで、お客さんから（　　　）を言われてしまった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means **complaint**.

**Question Translation**
“Because the delivery was late, I received a **complaint** from the customer.”

**文句 (monku)** means "complaint." The phrase "文句を言う" (to complain) is standard.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **失礼 (shitsurei)** - "Rudeness."
2. **命令 (meirei)** - "Order/Command."
3. **否定 (hitei)** - "Denial."

The context "delivery was late" (mistake/inconvenience) naturally leads to a "complaint" from a customer, not an order or denial.
questions.expl_ko:

questions.stem: この問題を解決するために、何かよい方法を（　　　）人はいませんか。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It means **thought of/came up with**.

**Question Translation**
“Is there anyone who **came up with** a good method to solve this problem?”

**Explanation**
**思いつく (omoitsuku)** means "to think of," "to hit upon," or "to come up with" (an idea/solution).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **引き受けた (hikiuketa)** - "Undertook/Accepted" (a job).
3. **知り合った (shiriatta)** - "Got to know" (a person).
4. **取り替えた (torikaeta)** - "Exchanged/Replaced."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Collocations: 方法 (method) or アイデア (idea) are usually objects of 思いつく.
questions.expl_ko:

근거: 좋은 해결 방법을 생각해낸 사람을 찾는 문맥.
오답: 2 引き受けた(맡았다), 3 知り合った(알게 됐다), 4 取り替えた(바꿨다)는 어긋남.
포인트: 「思いつく」=아이디어가 떠오르다.

questions.stem: 私の家から駅までは（　　　）なので、駅まで歩くと疲れます。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It means **slope/hilly road**.

**Question Translation**
“The way from my house to the station is a **slope**, so I get tired when I walk to the station.”

**Explanation**
The key reason for getting "tired" (疲れます) is that the road is a **坂道 (sakamichi - slope/hill)**.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **通り道 (toorimichi)** - "Path/Route."
3. **片道 (katamichi)** - "One-way."
4. **近道 (chikamichi)** - "Shortcut." (A shortcut usually makes things easier, not more tiring).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Look for the logical reason. Walking is tiring -> Why? Distance or difficulty (slope). Only "slope" fits the options.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「坂道(さかみち)」 — 비탈길, 언덕길.
근거: 걸어가면 피곤한 이유 → 오르막 길.
오답: 2 通り道(통로), 3 片道(편도), 4 近道(지름길)은 피로 이유로 부적절.
포인트: 坂道=언덕길.

questions.stem:
この店はとても人気があり、店の前にはいつも、開店を待つ人の長い（　　　）が
できている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means **queue/line**.

**Question Translation**
“This shop is very popular, and there is always a long **line** of people waiting for it to open.”

**Explanation**
**列 (retsu)** means a line or queue of people/things. "列ができる" (a line forms) is a set phrase.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **帯 (obi)** - "Belt/Sash."
2. **線 (sen)** - "Line (drawn)/Wire."
4. **壁 (kabe)** - "Wall."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Don't confuse 線 (a drawn line, like on paper) with 列 (a physical queue of objects/people).
questions.expl_ko:

오답: 1 帯(띠), 2 線(선), 4 壁(벽)은 사람 무리에 부적절.
포인트: 「列ができる/並ぶ」가 정형.

questions.stem: 後でレポートを書くために、実験の結果をノートに（　　　）した。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means **recorded**.

**Question Translation**
“I **recorded** the experiment results in my notebook so I could write the report later.”

**Explanation**
**記録 (kiroku)** means "record" or "recording data." You record results in a notebook.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **申請 (shinsei)** - "Application" (e.g., for a visa).
3. **予報 (yohou)** - "Forecast" (e.g., weather).
4. **通知 (tsuuchi)** - "Notification/Notice."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Match the noun "Results" (結果) with the action. You don't apply for or forecast results that already happened; you record them.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「記録(きろく)」 — 기록.
근거: 나중에 리포트를 쓰기 위해 실험 결과를 노트에 기록.
오답: 1 申請(신청), 3 予報(예보), 4 通知(통지)는 문맥 어긋남.
포인트: 「結果を記録する」가 정형.

questions.stem: あの仕事は本当に苦労した。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means **was tough/hard**.

**Question Translation**
“I really **had a hard time** with that job.”

**Explanation**
**苦労する (kurou suru)** means "to suffer hardships" or "to struggle." This is synonymous with **大変だった** (it was tough/hard).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **危険だった** - "Was dangerous."
3. **つまらなかった** - "Was boring."
4. **やりたくなかった** - "Didn't want to do it."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Paraphrasing questions often replace specific words with broader, simpler N4/N5 words. 苦労 (hardship) -> 大変 (tough).
questions.expl_ko:

근거: 「苦労する」=고생하다 ≒ 「大変」.
오답: 1 危険(위험), 3 つまらない(지루한), 4 やりたくない(하기 싫은)는 의미 어긋남.
포인트: 苦労=고생·노력의 어려움.

questions.stem: 本田さんと佐藤さんは、部屋からそっと出ていった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It means **quietly**.

**Question Translation**
“Mr. Honda and Mr. Sato went out of the room **quietly/stealthily**.”

**Explanation**
**そっと (sotto)** means "softly," "quietly," or "stealthily" so as not to disturb. This matches **静かに** (quietly).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **にぎやかに** - "Lively/Noisily."
3. **急いで** - "In a hurry."
4. **二人で** - "Together/The two of them."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Onomatopoeia and mimetic words (like *sotto*, *bikkuri*, *gussuri*) are frequent targets in N3 paraphrasing sections.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「静かに」 — 조용히.
근거: 「そっと」=살그머니, 조용히.
오답: 2 にぎやかに(시끌벅적), 3 急いで(서둘러), 4 二人で(둘이서)는 어긋남.
포인트: そっと=남에게 들키지 않게 조용히.

questions.stem: 昨日、田中さんは早退したそうです。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means **went home early**.

**Question Translation**
“I heard Mr. Tanaka **left (work/school) early** yesterday.”

**Explanation**
**早退 (soutai)** means leaving early (before the scheduled time). It corresponds to **早く帰った**.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **早く起きた** - "Woke up early" (早起き).
2. **早く来た** - "Came early."
3. **早く寝た** - "Went to sleep early."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Analyze the Kanji: 早 (Early) + 退 (Retreat/Leave). "Retreating early" = Leaving early.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「早く帰った」 — 일찍 돌아갔다.
근거: 「早退」=정해진 시간보다 일찍 퇴근/하교.
오답: 1 早起き(일찍 일어남), 2 早く来た(일찍 옴), 3 早く寝た(일찍 잠)는 어긋남.
포인트: 早退=조퇴.

questions.stem: ここに来てから１週間くらいたちました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means **passed**.

**Question Translation**
“About one week has **passed** since I came here.”

**Explanation**
**たつ (tatsu)** used with time means "to pass" or "to elapse." This matches **過ぎました (sugimashita - passed)**.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **待ちました** - "Waited."
2. **続けました** - "Continued."
3. **休みのした** - "Rested."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Be careful: *Tatsu* usually means "to stand" (立つ), but with time (時間/月日), it means "to pass" (経つ). Context determines the meaning.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「過ぎました」 — 지나갔다.
근거: 시간이 「たつ」(흐르다·경과하다) ≒ 「過ぎる」.
오답: 1 待つ(기다리다), 2 続ける(계속하다), 3 休む(쉬다)는 시간 경과와 무관.
포인트: 「時間が経つ/過ぎる」 동의.

questions.stem: この店ではさまざまな料理が食べられます。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means **various**.

**Question Translation**
“You can eat **various** dishes at this restaurant.”

**Explanation**
**さまざまな (samazama)** means "various" or "a variety of." It is a direct synonym for **いろいろな (iroiro)**.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **きれいな** - "Clean/Beautiful."
2. **有名な** - "Famous."
4. **特別な** - "Special."

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
*Samazama* is slightly more formal than *Iroiro*, but they are interchangeable in meaning. Memorize them as a pair.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「いろいろな」 — 여러 가지의.
근거: 「さまざまな」=다양한 ≒ 「いろいろな」.
오답: 1 きれい(깨끗·예쁨), 2 有名(유명), 4 特別(특별)은 동의어 아님.
포인트: さまざま=様々, 다양함.

questions.stem: 減少
opts: 弟はサッカーの試合に負けてから、元気が減少
調査によると、この国の人口は今後減少
今年はりんごの値段が去年より減少
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct.

**Word Translation**
減少（げんしょう） — decrease, reduction, decline

**Correct Sentence Translation**
According to a survey, this country's population is expected to **decrease** in the future.

**Explanation**
The word **減少** is used for the decrease of objective numbers or quantities (like population, accidents, volume). "人口が減少する" is a standard phrase.

**Other Options**

① 元気が減少している。
→ Energy has decreased. ❌ Incorrect Usage
For human energy/spirits, use "なくなる" or "落ち込む". *Genshou* is too clinical.

② 気温が減少している。
→ Temperature is decreasing. ❌ Incorrect Usage
For temperature, use "下がる (sagaru)".

④ 値段が減少した。
→ Price decreased. ❌ Incorrect Usage
For prices, use "下がる" or "安くなる".

**Usage Note**
Antonym: **増加** (zouka - increase).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
In Usage questions, distinguish between "objective data" (population, weight -> 減少) and "natural phenomena/scales" (temperature, price -> 下がる).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 — 「この国の人口は今後減少(する)」(이 나라의 인구가 앞으로 감소).
근거: 「減少」는 인구·수량 등 객관적 수치의 감소에 사용.
오답: 1 元気(기력)은 「減少」 안 씀, 2 気温은 「下がる」, 4 値段은 「下がる/安くなる」가 자연스러움.
포인트: 減少=수치·통계의 감소.

questions.stem: 重大
opts: これは祖母からもらった重大
外国での一人暮らしは、重大
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct.

**Word Translation**
重大（じゅうだい） — serious, grave, significant, major

**Correct Sentence Translation**
I hear there will be a **major** announcement from the president tomorrow.

**Explanation**
**重大** describes something of great consequence, often implying a heavy impact or seriousness (e.g., major news, grave error). "重大な発表" is a natural collocation.

**Other Options**

① 重大な顔
→ Serious face. 🔶 Awkward Usage
For expressions, use "真剣な (shinken)" or "まじめな".

② 重大な指輪
→ Serious/Grave ring. ❌ Incorrect Usage
For treasured items, use "大切な (taisetsu)" or "大事な".

③ 重大な経験
→ Grave experience. ❌ Incorrect Usage
For valuable experiences, use "貴重な (kichou)" or "大きな".

**Usage Note**
Common phrases: 重大な責任 (grave responsibility), 重大なミス (serious error).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
"Important" has many translations. 大切 (emotional value), 重要 (objective importance), 重大 (serious consequence/gravity). Match the nuance to the noun.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 — 「社長から重大(な発表/な話)」(사장의 중대한 발표).
근거: 「重大」=중대한·심각한 영향력. 발표·사건 등에 쓰임.
오답: 1 아이들에게는 부적합, 2 할머니의 물건은 「大切」, 3 일인생활은 「大変」.
포인트: 重大な発表/事件/責任 등.

questions.stem: ばらばら
opts: 冷凍庫に入れるのを忘れて、アイスクリームがばらばら
床に落としてしまい、資料がばらばら
ドアを開けたので、部屋の暖かい空気がばらばら
コップが倒れて、中のジュースがばらばら
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct.

**Word Translation**
ばらばら — scattered, in pieces, disorganized

**Correct Sentence Translation**
I dropped them on the floor, and the documents became **scattered**.

**Explanation**
**ばらばら** describes solid objects being scattered or disordered (like papers, parts of a machine). It fits papers spreading across a floor.

**Other Options**

① アイスクリームがばらばら
→ Ice cream scattered. ❌ Incorrect Usage
Melting is "溶ける (tokeru)".

③ 空気がばらばら
→ Air scattered. ❌ Incorrect Usage
Air escaping is "逃げる (nigeru)".

④ ジュースがばらばら
→ Juice scattered. ❌ Incorrect Usage
Liquids spilling is "こぼれる".

**Usage Note**
Also used for abstract concepts like opinions: "意見がばらばらだ" (Opinions are split).

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Mimetic words are specific to states of matter. *Barabara* = solids separating. *Betobeto* = sticky. *Boroboro* = crumbling/worn out.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 — 「床に落として、資料がばらばら(になった)」(자료가 흩어졌다).
근거: 「ばらばら」=낱낱이 흩어진 모습. 종이·부품 등 고체에 사용.
오답: 1 아이스크림 녹음은 「とける」, 3 공기 흩어짐은 「逃げる」, 4 액체 쏟김은 「こぼれる」.
포인트: ばらばら=흩어짐·분산.

questions.stem: 皮
opts: 机にぶつけて、パソコンの皮
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct.

**Word Translation**
皮（かわ） — skin, peel

**Correct Sentence Translation**
Boil the potatoes as they are without peeling the **skin**.

**Explanation**
**皮** refers to the skin of fruits, vegetables, or animals. "皮をむく" (to peel) is a fixed phrase.

**Other Options**

② パソコンの皮
→ PC's skin. ❌ Incorrect Usage
For hard surfaces of machines, use "表面 (hyoumen)" or "本体".

③ 卵の皮
→ Egg skin. ❌ Incorrect Usage
Eggshells are "殻 (から - kara)".

④ テキストの皮
→ Textbook skin. ❌ Incorrect Usage
Book covers are "表紙 (ひょうし)".

**Usage Note**
Kanji distinction: 皮 (skin/peel), 革 (processed leather), 川 (river). All are *kawa*.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Common trap: mixing up "Shell" (Kara) and "Skin" (Kawa). Eggs and crabs have *Kara*. Apples and potatoes have *Kawa*.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 — 「じゃがいもは皮(をむかずに)」(감자는 껍질째).
근거: 「皮」=과일·채소·동물의 껍질.
오답: 2 PC는 껍질 없음, 3 달걀은 「殻(から)」, 4 텍스트는 「表紙」.
포인트: 「皮をむく」가 정형. 단단한 껍데기는 「殻」.

questions.stem: オーダー
opts: 健康のため、毎日運動をするように医者からオーダー
母は妹に部屋の掃除をオーダー
レストランで食事の後にデザートをオーダー
次の大会で必ず優勝してほしいと選手たちにオーダー
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct.

**Word Translation**
オーダー (order) — ordering (food), custom-made request

**Correct Sentence Translation**
I **ordered** dessert after the meal at the restaurant.

**Explanation**
In Japanese, **オーダー** is primarily used for ordering food at a restaurant or ordering custom-made items (like a suit). It is NOT used for giving commands/instructions in daily life.

**Other Options**

① 医者からオーダーされた。
→ Ordered by doctor. ❌ Incorrect Usage
Doctors give "注意 (advice)" or "指示 (instructions)".

② 掃除をオーダーした。
→ Ordered to clean. ❌ Incorrect Usage
Requests are "頼む" or "言いつける".

④ 選手たちにオーダーした。
→ Ordered players. ❌ Incorrect Usage
Coaches give "指示 (instructions)".

**Usage Note**
Japanese Loanwords (Katakana) often have narrower meanings than the original English.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
When you see a Katakana word that comes from English, ask yourself: "Does Japanese use this ONLY in specific situations?" *Order* = Restaurants/Tailors. *Sign* (サイン) = Autographs (not street signs).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 — 「レストランでデザートをオーダー(する)」(식당에서 디저트 주문).
근거: 「オーダー」=음식 주문, 맞춤 제작.
오답: 1 의사 지시는 「指示」, 2 청소 시키기는 「言う/命じる」, 4 선수 요청은 「要求/お願い」.
포인트: オーダー=주문(외래어).

questions.stem: 先月東京で行われたサッカーの国際大会には、世界中（　　　）多くのサッカーファンが集まった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means "from".

**Question Translation**
"Many soccer fans gathered **from** all over the world for the international soccer tournament held in Tokyo last month."

**Explanation**
The sentence describes the origin or source from which the fans gathered. The particle **から** (kara) marks the starting point or source, meaning "from".

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **に** (ni) — indicates a destination or target.
   → Incorrect because the fans gathered *to* Tokyo, but they came *from* the world.
2. **で** (de) — indicates a location of action or means.
   → "Gathered *at* the world" doesn't make sense.
4. **まで** (made) — indicates a limit or endpoint ("until" or "as far as").
   → Incorrect context.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**〜から集まる** (atsumaru) = to gather from [place].

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
When you see verbs of movement or gathering (来る, 集まる), check if the noun indicates the *source* (requires から) or the *destination* (requires に/へ).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「から」 — ~에서, ~로부터.
근거: 「世界中から」=전 세계로부터(출발점·기원).
오답: 1 に(목적·도착점), 2 で(장소), 4 まで(도착점).
포인트: から=기원·출발점 표시.

questions.stem: 食事のマナーは国（　　　）違うので、私は旅行に行く前に、気をつけたほうがいいことはないか調べるようにしている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It means "depending on".

**Question Translation**
"Since dining manners differ **depending on** the country, I try to check if there is anything I should be careful about before traveling."

**Explanation**
The grammar pattern **〜によって** (ni yotte) is used to express variety or difference corresponding to a noun. Here, it means "varies by country" or "depends on the country".

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **に対して** (ni taishite) — "towards" or "in contrast to".
   → Used for opposition or direction of action.
3. **にとって** (ni totte) — "for" or "to".
   → Used for stating an opinion from a standpoint (e.g., "For me, this is important").
4. **において** (ni oite) — "in" or "at".
   → Formal marker for place or time.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**[Noun]によって違う** = "differs depending on [Noun]" is a very common N3 collocation.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「によって」 — ~에 따라.
근거: 나라별로 다른 점을 나타냄.
오답: 2 に対して(대해), 3 にとって(에게 있어), 4 において(에서)는 의미 어긋남.
포인트: 「Nによって違う/異なる」가 정형.

questions.stem: Ａ「もうすぐ（　　　）この前言ってたけど、準備は進んでる？」Ｂ「うん。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means "(that) you are moving".

**Question Translation**
A: "You were saying the other day **that** you're moving soon—is the preparation going well?"
B: "Yeah."

**Explanation**
The speaker is quoting what was said previously. In casual spoken Japanese, the quotation particle **と** (to) often becomes **って** (tte). So, 「引っ越すと」 becomes 「引っ越すって」.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **引っ越す** (hikkosu) — Missing the particle to connect to 「言ってた」 (said).
2. **引っ越すか** (hikkosu ka) — "Whether I will move".
   → Incorrect context; it's a quote of a fact, not a question.
4. **引っ越すのを** (hikkosu no o) — "The act of moving (object)".
   → You "say" a quote (と), you don't "say" an object (を) in this structure.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**Casual Quotation:** [Plain Form] + って言ってた (tte itteta) = "Said that..."
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「引っ越すって」 — 이사한다고.
근거: 회화체에서 「と言ってた」=「って言ってた」 인용.
오답: 1 동사 그대로는 부자연, 2 か(의문), 4 のを(목적격)는 부적합.
포인트: 「って」=구어체 인용 조사.

questions.stem: 田中「山下さん、映画をよく見るそうですね。映画館で見るんですか。」山下「映画館にも行きますが、（　　　）、家でＤＶＤを見ることが多いですね。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means "if I had to choose" or "rather".

**Question Translation**
Tanaka: "Mr. Yamashita, I hear you watch movies often. Do you watch them in theaters?"
Yamashita: "I do go to theaters, but **if anything / rather**, I watch DVDs at home more often."

**Explanation**
**どちらかというと** (dochira ka to iu to) is used when leaning towards one side of a comparison or when making a vague preference. It fits perfectly here to soften the contrast between going to theaters and the preferred habit of watching at home.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **確かに** (tashika ni) — "Certainly / Indeed".
   → Doesn't fit the flow of "but... more often".
2. **まるで** (marude) — "Just like / As if".
   → Requires a simile comparison (e.g., "like a dream").
3. **必ずしも** (kanarazu shimo) — "Not necessarily".
   → Used with negatives (e.g., "not necessarily true").

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**〜かというと** patterns are useful for explaining nuances. **どちらかというと** helps avoid definitive statements.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「どちらかというと」 — 어느 쪽인가 하면.
근거: 두 가지 중 한쪽으로 기울 때 쓰는 표현.
오답: 1 確かに(확실히), 2 まるで(마치), 3 必ずしも(반드시 ~인 것은 아닌)는 어긋남.
포인트: どちらかというと=상대적 선호 표명.

questions.stem:
（料理番組で）
「じゃがいもは、ゆでてから皮をむいたほうが、きれいに皮がむけます。ゆでた後、熱い（　　　）皮をむきましょう。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means "while".

**Question Translation**
"Potatoes peel more cleanly if you peel them after boiling. After boiling, let's peel them **while** they are hot."

**Explanation**
**うちに** (uchi ni) means "while (a state holds true)" or "before (a state changes)". Here, "hot while" (熱いうちに) implies you should do the action before they get cold.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **のに** (no ni) — "Even though" or "In order to".
   → "Even though it's hot" makes no sense here.
3. **ことで** (koto de) — "By doing..." or "Due to...".
   → Grammatically incorrect connection with an adjective here.
4. **とすると** (to suru to) — "If we assume...".
   → Incorrect conditional usage.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
Common phrase: **冷めないうちに** (before it gets cold) or **若いうちに** (while young).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「うちに」 — ~동안에.
근거: 「熱いうちに」=뜨거울 때, 상태가 유지되는 동안.
오답: 1 のに(~인데도), 3 ことで(~함으로써), 4 とすると(~라면)는 어긋남.
포인트: 「Aうちに」=A 상태가 변하기 전에.

questions.stem: 少し前にご飯を食べた（　　　）なのに、もうおなかがすいてきた。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means "just (did)".

**Question Translation**
"Even though I **just** ate a little while ago, I'm already getting hungry."

**Explanation**
**たばかり** (ta bakari) expresses that an action was completed very recently. The pattern is [Verb Ta-form] + ばかり.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **せい** (sei) — "Fault / Because of".
   → "Because I ate" doesn't fit with "Even though" (なのに).
2. **とき** (toki) — "Time / When".
   → "Even though it is the time I ate" is unnatural.
3. **よう** (you) — "Seem like".
   → "Even though it seems I ate" is odd contextually.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**〜たばかりなのに** is a strong pattern expressing surprise that despite just doing X, Y happened.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「ばかり」 — 막 ~한 참.
근거: 「食べたばかり」=막 먹은 직후.
오답: 1 せい(탓), 2 とき(때), 3 よう(~듯)은 의미 어긋남.
포인트: 「Vたばかり」=동작 직후.

questions.stem: 私は小学生のころ、ゲームが大好きだった。早く家でゲームが（　　　）、毎日学校から走って帰っていた。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It means "wanted to do... and so".

**Question Translation**
"When I was in elementary school, I loved video games. I **wanted to play** games at home so badly/quickly, (so) I ran home from school every day."

**Explanation**
The speaker explains the *reason* for running home. The te-form of the desiderative (**〜たい** → **〜たくて**) expresses a cause/reason involving feelings. "Because I wanted to play..."

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **したくても** (shitakute mo) — "Even if I wanted to do".
   → Incorrect logic.
3. **するためで** (suru tame de) — "For the purpose of doing".
   → Grammatically awkward connection; `ため` usually connects directly or with `に` in this context (`ゲームをするために`). Also `が` marks the object, which fits `〜たい` better than `〜する`.
4. **するためでも** (suru tame demo) — "Even for the purpose of doing".

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
Note the particle **が**: **ゲームがしたい** (I want to play games). `が` marks the object of desire.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「したくて」 — 하고 싶어서.
근거: 「〜たい」 + 「て」=이유 표현. 게임이 하고 싶어서 뛰어옴.
오답: 2 ても(역접), 3·4 ためで는 잘못된 형태.
포인트: 「Vたくて」=원해서 + 그 이유로.

questions.stem: 弟の誕生日プレゼントを何にするか考えている。何を（　　　）、喜ぶだろうか。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means "if I give".

**Question Translation**
"I'm thinking about what to get for my younger brother's birthday present. I wonder what he would be happy **if I gave him**."

**Explanation**
The speaker is giving to the brother. Giving to someone else is **あげる**. The conditional **〜たら** (if) fits the "wondering" context.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **くれたら** (kuretara) — "If he gives me".
   → Direction is wrong (brother giving to speaker).
3. **くれるから** (kureru kara) — "Because he gives me".
   → Wrong direction and meaning.
4. **あげるから** (ageru kara) — "Because I will give him".
   → Doesn't fit with `喜ぶだろうか` (will he be happy?)

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
Giving/Receiving verbs are crucial. **あげる** = Outward (I give to him). **くれる** = Inward (He gives to me).

포인트: あげる=나→남, くれる=남→나.

questions.stem: （電話で）Ａ「この前借りた小説、もう１回貸してもらえない？」Ｂ「うん、いいよ。あした、学校に（　　　）ね。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means "I will bring it (go with it)".

**Question Translation**
A: "Can you lend me that novel I borrowed before one more time?"
B: "Yeah, sure. I'll **bring it** to school tomorrow."

**Explanation**
B is going to school carrying the book. The verb is **持っていく** (motte iku - to take/bring along). In conversation, `〜ていく` often shortens to **〜てく**. So `持っていく` becomes **持ってく**.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **持っとく** (mottoku) — Short for `持っておく` (hold on to it / keep it for later).
   → Doesn't imply moving it to school.
2. **持ってる** (motteru) — Short for `持っている` (to have / to be holding).
   → Stative; doesn't describe the action of bringing it tomorrow.
4. **持っちゃう** (mocchau) — Short for `持ってしまう` (to hold it completely / unintentionally).

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**Contractions in Speech:**
ていく → てく
ておく → とく
ている → てる

오답: 1 持っとく(둬두다), 2 持ってる(가지고 있다), 4 持っちゃう(저질러 가지다)는 문맥 어긋남.
포인트: 「持っていく」→「持ってく」 구어 축약.

**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means "I hope it clears up."

**Question Translation**
A: "I'm looking forward to the mountain climbing the week after next."
B: "Yeah, **I hope the weather is nice**."

**Explanation**
The grammar pattern **〜といい** (to ii) expresses a hope or wish for something to happen. "It would be good if it clears up."

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **晴れてきた** (harete kita) — "It has started to clear up."
   → Incorrect because the trip is in the future.
3. **晴れるんだ** (hareru n da) — "It is going to be sunny!" (Explanatory/Assertive).
   → Too definitive for a future prediction usually, lacks the nuance of "hope".
4. **晴れてよかった** (harete yokatta) — "I'm glad it cleared up."
   → Past tense; incorrect for a future event.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**[Verb-Dictionary] + といい** = I hope / It would be nice if.
Also seen as: **〜といいですね**.

오답: 1 晴れてきた(이미 맑아짐), 3 晴れるんだ(단정), 4 晴れてよかった(이미 일어남)는 시제·태도 어긋남.

questions.stem:
（市のホームページで）
９月３０日に絵本作家の坂木みどりさんによる講演会が開かれます。市民の方であれば、どなたでも無料で（　　　）。
opts: ご参加になっています
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It means "you can participate".

**Question Translation**
"A lecture by picture book author Midori Sakaki will be held on September 30th. Any citizen **can participate** for free."

**Explanation**
The text addresses citizens (customers/respected others), so respectful language (Keigo) is used. The pattern **ご〜になる** is respectful. Adding potential ability, it becomes **ご〜になれる**. Thus, **ご参加になれます** means "you are able to participate" (Respectful).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **参加いたします** (sanka itashimasu) — Humble language.
   → The city would use this for *their own* actions, not the citizens'.
3. **ご参加になっています** (go sanka ni natte imasu) — "You are participating" (Current state/result).
   → Doesn't fit the context of inviting people to a future event ability.
4. **参加できております** (sanka dekite orimasu) — "We are able to participate" (Humble).
   → Wrong subject.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**Respectful Potential:** ご + Noun + になれる.
Alternatively: 参加いただけます (You can participate - receiving favor).

오답: 2 いたします(겸양), 3 ご〜になっています(진행), 4 참가できております(부자연)는 부적합.

questions.stem: 以前は、会社の窓から海を見ることができたが、隣に高いビルができて、（　　　）。
opts: 見えなくてもよくなった
見られたかもしれない
見えなくなってしまった
見られるようにならない
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means "it ended up becoming invisible".

**Question Translation**
"Before, I could see the sea from the office window, but a tall building was built next door, and **I can't see it anymore / it has become invisible**."

**Explanation**
The speaker describes a change in state from visible to invisible. **見えなくなる** (to become invisible/unable to see). The **〜てしまった** form adds a nuance of regret or finality (unfortunately).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **見えなくてもよくなった** — "It became okay not to see it."
   → Context suggests the speaker liked the view, not that they stopped caring.
2. **見られたかもしれない** — "Might have been able to be seen."
   → Guessing about the past; doesn't fit the result of the new building.
4. **見られるようにならない** — "Does not become able to be seen."
   → Incorrect grammar/tense for the result.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**見える** (potential not needed) vs **見られる** (active potential). For scenery being visible naturally, **見える** is standard. Change of state: **見えなくなる**.

근거: 빌딩이 생겨 바다가 안 보이는 상태 변화+유감.

포인트: 「〜なくなってしまう」=원치 않는 상태 변화.

questions.stem: （病院で）医者「この薬を五日間飲んでください。」患者「わかりました。熱が下がっても（　　　）。」医者「はい。五日間必ず飲んでください。」
opts: 飲んではいけませんか
飲んでいないんでしょうか
飲んでみようと思いますか
飲んだほうがいいでしょうか
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means "should I drink it?".

**Question Translation**
Doctor: "Please take this medicine for five days."
Patient: "Understood. Even if my fever goes down, **should I (still) take it?**"
Doctor: "Yes. Please make sure to take it for five days."

**Explanation**
The patient is asking for advice or instruction. **〜たほうがいいですか** (Is it better to... / Should I...?) is the correct pattern here.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **飲んではいけませんか** — "Must I not drink it?"
   → Asking if it's prohibited, which conflicts with the context of being prescribed medicine.
2. **飲んでいないんでしょうか** — "Am I not drinking it?"
   → Asking about current state, doesn't make sense.
3. **飲んでみようと思いますか** — "Do you think I will try to drink it?"
   → Asking the doctor about the patient's own volition; nonsensical.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**Advice Pattern:** [Verb Ta-form] + ほうがいい (It is better to / You should).

오답: 1 飲んではいけませんか(금지 확인은 어색), 2 飲んでいないんでしょうか(부자연), 3 飲んでみよう(의지)는 부적합.
포인트: 「〜たほうがいい」=조언 청구·제시.

questions.stem: 今年の夏も暑いが、＿＿＿　＿＿＿　★　＿＿＿　ずっと涼しく感じる。
questions.expl:
**Answer (★ Position)**
Answer 4: 去年の夏に

**Correct Order**
1 → 3 → 4 → 2 📌 異常な → 暑さだった → 去年の夏に → 比べると

**Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown**
① 異常な — "abnormal" (Adjective modifying noun)
③ 暑さだった — "was heat" (Noun phrase + copula)
④ 去年の夏に — "to last summer" (Target of comparison)
② 比べると — "when compared" (Verb phrase)

**Reconstructed Sentence**
今年の夏も暑いが、**異常な暑さだった去年の夏に比べると**ずっと涼しく感じる。
→ "This summer is hot too, but **compared to last summer, which was abnormally hot**, it feels much cooler."

**Why This Order Works**
1. **異常な** modifies **暑さ** (abnormal heat). -> `異常な暑さだった` (It was abnormal heat).
2. The phrase `異常な暑さだった` modifies **去年の夏** (Last summer, which was abnormal heat).
3. **〜に比べると** is the comparison pattern "Compared to ~".
4. Thus: [異常な暑さだった -> 去年の夏] + に + 比べると.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Identify the main grammar structure first: **〜に比べると** (compared to). Then find the noun that attaches to `に`. Here, it's `去年の夏`. Then see what's left to modify that noun.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 (★자리=세 번째 「去年の夏に」). 올바른 순서: 異常な→暑さだった→去年の夏に→比べると.
포인트: 「異常な暑さだった去年の夏に比べると」(이상한 더위였던 작년 여름에 비하면) — 「Nに比べると」=N에 비하면.

questions.stem: 初めてアルバイトをして、お金を稼ぐ　＿＿＿　＿＿＿　★　＿＿＿　わかった。
questions.expl:
**Answer (★ Position)**
Answer 2: 大変な

**Correct Order**
3 → 4 → 2 → 1 📌 ことが → どれだけ → 大変な → ことか

**Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown**
③ ことが — "(the act of) earning money" (Topic/Subject)
④ どれだけ — "how much / to what extent"
② 大変な — "difficult / hard" (Adjective)
① ことか — "thing it is" (Exclamation/Question marker)

**Reconstructed Sentence**
初めてアルバイトをして、お金を稼ぐ**ことが、どれだけ大変なことか**わかった。
→ "I worked part-time for the first time and understood **just how difficult it is to earn money**."

**Why This Order Works**
1. **お金を稼ぐ** connects to **ことが** to make the topic clause.
2. **どれだけ** signals an embedded question regarding degree, usually paired with an adjective.
3. **大変な** modifies **こと**.
4. **ことか** ends the embedded question clause: "how difficult a thing it is".

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Pattern: **どれだけ + [Adjective] + ことか**. This expresses "How [Adjective] it is!" or "I realized how [Adjective] it is."
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 (★자리=세 번째 「大変な」). 올바른 순서: ことが→どれだけ→大変な→ことか.
포인트: 「どれだけ〜ことか」=얼마나 ~인지(감탄). 「お金を稼ぐことがどれだけ大変なことか」.

questions.stem: 今日、昼食の後、図書館へ行って本を読んでいたら　＿＿＿　＿＿＿　★　＿＿＿　　。
questions.expl:
**Answer (★ Position)**
Answer 1: ので

**Correct Order**
4 → 2 → 1 → 3 📌 いつのまにか → 外が暗くなっていた → ので → 驚いた

**Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown**
④ いつのまにか — "before I knew it / unnoticed"
② 外が暗くなっていた — "outside had become dark"
① ので — "because / so" (Reason marker)
③ 驚いた — "was surprised" (Final verb)

**Reconstructed Sentence**
...本を読んでいたら、**いつのまにか外が暗くなっていたので驚いた**。
→ "...I was reading a book, and I was surprised **because it had become dark outside before I realized it**."

**Why This Order Works**
1. **いつのまにか** is an adverb that modifies the change in state (**暗くなっていた**).
2. **外が暗くなっていた** describes the situation.
3. **ので** connects the situation as the reason for the feeling.
4. **驚いた** is the reaction.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Find the Subject/Cause and Result. Cause: "Got dark". Result: "Surprised". Connector: "Node". Order: Cause -> Node -> Result.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 (★자리=세 번째 「ので」). 올바른 순서: いつのまにか→外が暗くなっていた→ので→驚いた.
포인트: 「いつのまにか」(어느새)+상태 변화+「ので」(이유)+감정 결과.

questions.stem: このチーズケーキの　＿＿＿　＿＿＿　★　＿＿＿　、誰でも簡単に作れると思う。
questions.expl:
**Answer (★ Position)**
Answer 3: だけ

**Correct Order**
2 → 4 → 3 → 1 📌 作り方は → 材料を混ぜて冷やす → だけ → だから

**Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown**
② 作り方は — "As for the way to make (it)"
④ 材料を混ぜて冷やす — "mix ingredients and chill"
③ だけ — "just / only"
① だから — "because (it is)"

**Reconstructed Sentence**
このチーズケーキの**作り方は、材料を混ぜて冷やすだけだから**、誰でも簡単に作れると思う。
→ "**Because the way to make** this cheesecake **is just mixing ingredients and chilling**, I think anyone can make it easily."

**Why This Order Works**
1. **このチーズケーキの** connects to **作り方は** (Topic).
2. The method is described: **材料を混ぜて冷やす**.
3. **だけ** attaches to the description to mean "just doing that".
4. **だから** gives the reason for the next clause ("anyone can make it").

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Look for the predicate structure: Topic + wa + Description + dake + da/dakara.

정답: 3번 (★자리=세 번째 「だけ」). 올바른 순서: 作り方は→材料を混ぜて冷やす→だけ→だから.
포인트: 「Vるだけ」(~하기만 하면 됨)+「だから」(이유)=간단함을 설명.

questions.stem: Ａ「あさってのサッカーの試合、応援に行くね。天気予報では曇りと言っていたけど、雨だったら中止になる？」Ｂ「ありがとう。もし　＿＿＿　＿＿＿　★　＿＿＿　応援には無理して来なくてもいいよ。」
opts: 中止にならないんだけど
questions.expl:
Correct Order 3 → 2 → 4 → 1 📌 雨が降ったとしても → 試合は → 中止にならないんだけど → 雨が降ったら

Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ③ 雨が降ったとしても — "Even if it rains" ② 試合は — "as for the match" (Subject) ④ 中止にならないんだけど — "it won't be cancelled, but" ① 雨が降ったら — "if it rains"

Reconstructed Sentence もし雨が降ったとしても、試合は中止にならないんだけど、雨が降ったら応援には無理して来なくてもいいよ。 → "Even if it rains, the match won't be cancelled, but if it rains, you don't have to force yourself to come cheer."

Why This Order Works

Answering A: A asked, "Will it be cancelled if it rains?" B must answer that concern first. The strongest denial is "Even if it rains (3), the match (2) won't be cancelled (4)."

The Logical Conclusion: The sentence ends with "...you don't have to come." Logically, this permission is granted on the condition that it rains. Therefore, "If it rains" (1) must go at the very end to connect to the final clause.

The "Moshi" Trap: While "Moshi" often starts a sentence, it applies to the entire scenario. It frames the hypothetical situation of the match not being cancelled despite rain.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 (★자리=세 번째 「中止にならないんだけど」). 올바른 순서: 雨が降ったとしても→試合は→中止にならないんだけど→雨が降ったら.
포인트: 「〜としても」(설령 ~라도)+역접 「けど」+조건 「たら」의 흐름.

questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means "Even so" or "Nevertheless".

**Question Translation**
(Context) "...It was very cold and I wanted to stay in a warm place. **[19]**, thinking 'I came all this way', I started practicing skiing."

**Explanation**
The speaker describes a negative situation (very cold, wanted to be warm) but then describes an action that goes against that feeling (started practicing). **それでも** (soredemo) connects a negative context with a positive or contradictory action ("Even though it was cold...").

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **例えば** (tatoeba) — "For example".
3. **実は** (jitsu wa) — "Actually / To tell the truth".
4. **また** (mata) — "Also / Again".

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
Conjunctions are key in Text Grammar. Look at the sentence before and after the blank. [Negative feeling] -> [Positive action] requires an adversative conjunction like `しかし`, `でも`, or `それでも`.
questions.expl_ko:

근거: 추워서 따뜻한 곳에 있고 싶었지만, 「그래도」 일부러 왔으니까 스키 연습 시작 — 역접.
오답: 1 例えば(예시), 3 実は(사실), 4 また(또)는 문맥 어긋남.
포인트: それでも=역접 접속사.

opts: 滑れるようになりました
滑れるようにしてあげました
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It means "I became able to ski (slide)".

**Question Translation**
"In half a day, I **became able to ski** without falling much."

**Explanation**
The sentence describes a change in the speaker's potential ability. The pattern **Potential Verb + ようになる** (to reach the point where one can...) fits perfectly. **滑れる** (can slide/ski) + **ようになりました**.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. **〜てあげました** — "I did it for someone else."
   → The speaker is talking about their own progress.
3. **〜てくれました** — "Someone did it for me."
4. **〜てもらえました** — "I received the favor of someone doing it."

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**Change of State:**
Potential Verb + ようになる (e.g., 泳げるようになる - become able to swim).
Negative Potential: 泳げなくなる (become unable to swim).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「滑れるようになりました」 — 탈 수 있게 됐다.
근거: 반나절 만에 능력 변화(가능형+ようになる).
오답: 2 してあげる(은혜 베풂), 3 滑ってくれた(타 주다·방향 부적합), 4 もらえた(부자연).
포인트: 「Vれるようになる」=가능 상태로 변화.

questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means "that (specific)".

**Question Translation**
"Now, whenever I see ski ads at the station, I remember **that** ski trip."

**Explanation**
The speaker is referring to a specific past memory (the trip described in the text). In a monologue or essay, **あの** is used to refer to something familiar to the speaker or a specific past event ingrained in their memory ("that trip I just told you about").

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **あの広告の** — "That ad's (ski trip)".
   → The trip didn't belong to the ad; the ad triggers the memory of the *actual* trip.
2. **ああいう** — "That kind of / Like that".
   → Implies a general category, but the speaker remembers the *specific* trip they took.
3. **ある** — "A certain".
   → Used to introduce something new or indefinite.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
**Ko-So-A-Do words in writing:**
**その** refers to something just mentioned.
**あの** refers to something familiar to both writer and reader, or a nostalgic memory for the writer.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「あの」 — 그(특정 기억).
근거: 본인의 경험을 회상하며 가리킬 때 「あの」.
오답: 1 あの広告の(광고의), 2 ああいう(그런 종류), 3 ある(어떤)는 특정 경험 회상에 부적합.
포인트: 회상 속 특정 대상 → 「あの」.

questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means "I understood / realized".

**Question Translation**
"Having tried skiing myself, I **understood** well the reason why skiing has become a representative winter sport in Japan."

**Explanation**
The speaker is stating a realization they had *after* the experience. **わかりました** (I understood/realized) indicates the completion of this understanding based on the trip.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. **わかっていきます** — "I will go on understanding."
   → Future continuous; doesn't fit the conclusion.
2. **わかるのでしょう** — "I suppose one would understand."
   → Conjecture; the speaker is stating their own confirmed feeling.
4. **わかったそうです** — "I heard that they understood."
   → Hearsay; incorrect for own feelings.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
Concluding an essay often involves stating a realization found through the experience, usually in the past tense (**〜とわかりました**) or present state (**〜とわかります**).

questions.stem: ジョンさんがこのメールで頼まれていることはどれか。
opts: 今週中にもう一度、資料のチェックをすること
今週中に資料のチェックをするために木村さんと会うこと
今週中に日本語の資料を英語に直すこと
今週中に新しい資料を作ること
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1 is correct. It means "To check the materials again by the end of this week."

**Question Translation**
"What is John asked to do in this email?"

**Explanation**
In the email, Kimura writes: "I am sending the corrected materials... so could you look at them one more time?" (直した資料を…送りますので、もう一度見てもらえませんか). Kimura also ends with: "Please reply by within this week" (今週中にご返信をお願いいたします). Therefore, John needs to check the materials again by the end of this week.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. 今週中に資料のチェックをするために木村さんと会うこと
(To meet Ms. Kimura by the end of this week to check the materials) ❌
→ Incorrect. The email says the materials are being "sent with this email" (このメールと一緒に送ります), not that they need to meet in person.

3. 今週中に日本語の資料を英語に直すこと
(To correct the Japanese materials into English by the end of this week) ❌
→ Incorrect. John previously checked the English materials, and now Kimura is asking him to check the *corrections* Kimura made. He is not asked to translate from Japanese.

4. 今週中に新しい資料を作ること
(To create new materials by the end of this week) ❌
→ Incorrect. Kimura has already created/fixed the graphs and text. John is only asked to *check* (見てもらえませんか) them, not create new ones from scratch.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Pay attention to the closing sentence of emails (usually `〜をお願いします` or `〜てください`). This is often where the specific "action item" or request is located.

오답: 2 木村와 만남, 3 영어로 번역, 4 새 자료 작성은 본문에 없음.
포인트: 부탁 내용=재확인+회신.

questions.stem: 駅ビルの特別窓口でできることは何か。
opts: 予約した本を借りることと、本を返すことと、本を読むことと、本の予約をすること
予約した本を借りることと、本を返すことと、本を読むこと
予約した本を借りることと、本を返すことと、本の予約をすること
予約した本を借りることと、本を返すことだけ
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means "Borrowing reserved books, returning books, and making book reservations."

**Question Translation**
"What can be done at the special counter in the station building?"

**Explanation**
The text lists the functions of the special counter: 
1. "You can borrow reserved books" (予約した本を借りること)
2. "You can return books" (本を返すことができます)
3. "Reservations... are also possible at the special counter" (特別窓口でも可能です).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 予約した本を借りることと、本を返すことと、本を読むことと、本の予約をすること
(Borrowing reserved books, returning books, reading books, and reserving books) ❌
→ Incorrect. The text explicitly states: "There is no space to read books or newspapers" (本や新聞を読む場所はありません).

2. 予約した本を借りることと、本を返すことと、本を読むこと
(Borrowing reserved books, returning books, and reading books) ❌
→ Incorrect. As mentioned above, reading is not allowed.

4. 予約した本を借りることと、本を返すことだけ
(Only borrowing reserved books and returning books) ❌
→ Incorrect. The text adds that reserving books is *also* possible (なお、...特別窓口でも可能です), so it is not "only" borrowing and returning.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
In "Notice" (お知らせ) questions, look for explicit exclusions (sentences with `〜はありません` or `できません`) and additions (sentences starting with `なお` or `また`). These usually determine the correct answer.

포인트: 읽는 장소는 없음=옵션 1 배제.

questions.stem: 予想と違っていて、意外だったとあるが、何が意外だったのか。
opts: 紙コップのように、一度使ったら捨てる紙の靴下があること
記事で紹介されていた靴下は、洗濯ができて、繰り返して使えること
記事で紹介されていた靴下は、よく汗を吸ってすぐに乾くこと
長い時間靴をはく人のための靴下があること
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means "The socks introduced in the article can be washed and used repeatedly."

**Question Translation**
"The text says 'It was different from my expectation and surprising.' What was surprising?"

**Explanation**
The author initially expected the paper socks to be disposable, "like paper cups, thrown away after one use" (一度使ったら捨ててしまう物なのだろう). However, the surprise was that despite being paper, they are "strong against water" and "can be washed and used many times" (洗濯をして何度も使える).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 紙コップのように、一度使ったら捨てる紙の靴下があること
(That there are paper socks that are thrown away after one use, like paper cups) ❌
→ Incorrect. This was the author's *initial expectation*, not the surprising reality. The reality was the opposite.

3. 記事で紹介されていた靴下は、よく汗を吸ってすぐに乾くこと
(That the socks introduced in the article absorb sweat well and dry quickly) ❌
→ Incorrect. While the text mentions this is a feature of the socks, the specific context of the "surprise" (indicated by `しかし` - however) was about the durability and reusability compared to the "disposable" expectation.

4. 長い時間靴をはく人のための靴下があること
(That there are socks for people who wear shoes for a long time) ❌
→ Incorrect. This is mentioned as a benefit, but it is not the reason for the author's surprise regarding the material's nature.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
When asked about a reaction like "surprising" (意外), look for the contrast marker (like `しかし`, `でも`, or `が`) immediately following the author's assumption. The surprise is usually the fact that *contradicts* the assumption.

questions.stem: この文章では、患者が「別のところが痛くなってきた。今度はそっちが悪いみたいだ。」と言うのは、なぜだと言っているか。
opts: 悪いところが治ってきたときに、複数の痛みを感じるようになることがあるから
悪いところが一つ治ると、次に悪かったところがもっと悪くなることがあるから
痛いところがいくつかあっても、患者は一つずつしか伝えないことがあるから
痛かったところがよくなると、別のところの痛みを感じるようになることがあるから
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means "Because when a painful area gets better, they start to feel pain in another area."

**Question Translation**
"In this text, why is it said that patients say 'Another place has started hurting. It seems that part is bad this time'?"

**Explanation**
The text explains that the human body only feels one pain (the strongest one) at a time (一つの痛みしか感じない). Therefore, when the most painful part heals (最も悪かったところが治ってきた), the patient begins to notice the *next* strongest pain that was masked before.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 悪いところが治ってきたときに、複数の痛みを感じるようになることがあるから
(Because when the bad part heals, they sometimes start to feel multiple pains) ❌
→ Incorrect. The text says the body feels *only one* pain at a time (strongest one), not multiple pains simultaneously.

2. 悪いところが一つ治ると、次に悪かったところがもっと悪くなることがあるから
(Because when one bad part heals, the next bad part sometimes gets worse) ❌
→ Incorrect. The second part didn't get *worse*; it just became noticeable because the first pain decreased.

3. 痛いところがいくつかあっても、患者は一つずつしか伝えないことがあるから
(Because even if there are several painful spots, patients sometimes only report them one by one) ❌
→ Incorrect. It is not about the patient *choosing* to report one by one, but about the body's mechanism of *feeling* only the strongest one.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
For scientific/medical explanation passages, trace the cause-and-effect chain carefully. Here: Strongest pain fades → Next strongest pain becomes perceptible. Avoid answers that imply the condition physically worsened (Option 2) or implies voluntary choice (Option 3).

questions.stem: ①例とあるが、何についての例か。
opts: 「私」と妹は、顔だけでなく、性格も似ているということ
「私」と妹は、嫌いな物や嫌なことに対する姿勢が逆だということ
「私」と妹は、協力し合って夏休みの宿題をしていたということ
「私」と妹は、毎年夏休みに必ず両親にしかられていたこと
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means "That 'I' and the sister have opposite attitudes toward things they dislike or find unpleasant."

**Question Translation**
"It says 'One more example' (①例). What is this an example of?"

**Explanation**
The paragraph preceding the example states: "Our attitudes toward disliked things or unpleasant things are opposite" (嫌いな物や嫌なことに対する姿勢は逆です). The text first gives the example of food, and then says "There is one more example" before discussing homework. Thus, homework is the second example of their opposite attitudes toward things they dislike.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 「私」と妹は、顔だけでなく、性格も似ているということ
(That 'I' and the sister are alike not only in face but also in personality) ❌
→ Incorrect. The text says their attitudes are *opposite* (逆), not alike.

3. 「私」と妹は、協力し合って夏休みの宿題をしていたということ
(That 'I' and the sister cooperated to do summer homework) ❌
→ Incorrect. There is no mention of cooperation; they worked separately with different styles.

4. 「私」と妹は、毎年夏休みに必ず両親にしかられていたこと
(That 'I' and the sister were definitely scolded by parents every summer vacation) ❌
→ Incorrect. Usually, *only* the author was scolded (私だけ両親にしかられました). The sister was only scolded that *one specific year*.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
When asked "What is this an example of?", look at the sentence immediately *preceding* the first example. The thesis statement usually comes before the illustrations (First example: food. Second example: homework. Thesis: Opposite attitudes).
questions.expl_ko:

근거: 직전 단락 「嫌いな物や嫌なことに対する姿勢は逆」 + 「もう一つ例があります」.
오답: 1 성격 닮음, 3 협력, 4 매년 혼남은 예시의 주제가 아님.
포인트: '싫은 것에 대한 자세의 차이'의 두 번째 예.

questions.stem: 「私」と妹は、毎年の夏休みの宿題をどう思っていたか。
opts: 「私」は好きだったが、妹は嫌だった。
「私」は嫌だったが、妹は好きだった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means "Both 'I' and the sister disliked it."

**Question Translation**
"What did 'I' and the sister think about the annual summer homework?"

**Explanation**
The text introduces the topic of homework as "another example" of their "attitude toward **disliked things/unpleasant things**" (嫌いな物や嫌なこと). This classification implies that for *both* of them, homework was a "disliked thing." They just handled the dislike differently (one by procrastinating, one by rushing to finish).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 「私」も妹も好きだった。
(Both 'I' and the sister liked it.) ❌
→ Incorrect. It is listed as a "disliked thing."

3. 「私」は好きだったが、妹は嫌だった。
('I' liked it, but the sister disliked it.) ❌
→ Incorrect.

4. 「私」は嫌だったが、妹は好きだった。
('I' disliked it, but the sister liked it.) ❌
→ Incorrect. Even though the sister finished it early, the text categorizes it under "disliked things" for both of them. Finishing early is just her way of dealing with the unpleasant task.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
This requires logical deduction based on the structure of the text. If X is an example of "Disliked Things," then the characters involved must dislike X, regardless of how efficiently they perform X.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 — 「私」도 동생도 싫어했다.
근거: 숙제 이야기가 「嫌いな物や嫌なこと」의 예로 제시됨. 동생은 일찍 끝내 버리고, 「私」는 막판에 시작 — 둘 다 싫었기 때문에 각자 다르게 회피.
오답: 1·3·4는 본문의 전제와 모순.
포인트: '싫은 것에 대한 자세의 예' = 둘 다 싫어함이 전제.

questions.stem: ②妹もしかられたとあるが、妹はなぜしかられたのか。
opts: 夏休みが終わる数日前になって宿題をやり始めたから
「私」の作文と同じことを「今年の夏休みの思い出」に書いたから
「今年の夏休みの思い出」に、実際にはしていないことを書いたから
「今年の夏休みの思い出」を、夏休みの最後の日まで書かなかったから
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means "Because she wrote about things she hadn't actually done yet in 'Memories of Summer Vacation'."

**Question Translation**
"It says 'The sister was also scolded' (②妹もしかられた). Why was the sister scolded?"

**Explanation**
The text explains that the sister finished all homework right at the start of summer. However, one assignment was "Memories of This Summer Vacation" (今年の夏休みの思い出). Since she wrote it at the beginning, she couldn't have experienced it yet. The mother realized the content was a lie (うそであることに気づいて) because she wrote about events that hadn't happened yet.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 夏休みが終わる数日前になって宿題をやり始めたから
(Because she started doing homework a few days before summer vacation ended) ❌
→ Incorrect. That is what the *author* usually did, not the sister.

2. 「私」の作文と同じことを「今年の夏休みの思い出」に書いたから
(Because she wrote the same things as 'I' did in 'Memories of Summer Vacation') ❌
→ Incorrect. There is no mention of copying the author's work.

4. 「今年の夏休みの思い出」を、夏休みの最後の日まで書かなかったから
(Because she didn't write 'Memories of Summer Vacation' until the last day of summer) ❌
→ Incorrect. She wrote it at the very beginning. She was made to *rewrite* it on the last day.

**💡 Test-Taking Tip**
Locate the exact sentence explaining the scolding. The text says "Mother realized the content was a lie." Connect "Lie" + "Wrote memories at the start of summer" -> She made up future events.
